prompt: Which pair best describes the two roles an operating system plays?
choices: Compiler and linker
Resource allocator and control program
Bootstrap loader and firmware
Shell and kernel
solution: As a resource allocator the OS decides who gets the CPU, memory, storage, and I/O devices — and for how long. As a control program it manages how user programs run so one misbehaving program can't take down the machine.

prompt: The kernel is the one program that is always running on the computer.
solution: True. The most common working definition of the OS is exactly this: the kernel is the one program running at all times while the machine is powered on. Everything else is a system program or an application program.

prompt: Where is the bootstrap program stored, and why?
choices: On the hard disk, because it is large
In main memory (RAM), because it is fast
In firmware (ROM/EPROM), because it must be reliable before any disk is read
In the CPU cache, because it runs first
solution: The bootstrap program must exist and work before anything else has loaded — including before a disk can be read — so it lives in firmware (ROM or EPROM) built into the hardware. Its job is to initialize the system and load the kernel.

prompt: Which storage medium can the CPU access directly?
choices: Hard-disk drive
Main memory (RAM)
Solid-state drive
Magnetic tape
solution: Main memory is the only large storage the CPU can access directly (random access). Secondary storage — SSD, hard disk, tape — is reached through the I/O system, not directly by CPU instructions.

prompt: Registers, cache, and main memory are all volatile — they lose their contents when power is removed.
solution: True. Everything from registers down through main memory is volatile. The volatile / nonvolatile boundary sits just below main memory: secondary storage (NVM/SSD, hard disk, optical, tape) is nonvolatile and survives a power cycle.

prompt: Reading down the storage hierarchy from fastest to slowest, which is the FIRST nonvolatile tier?
choices: Cache
Main memory (RAM)
Nonvolatile memory / SSD
CPU registers
solution: Registers → cache → main memory are all volatile. The nonvolatile secondary-storage tiers begin at nonvolatile memory / SSD, then hard disk, optical disk, and finally magnetic tape.

prompt: How many bits are in one byte?
solution: A byte is 8 bits. A bit is the smallest unit (a single 0 or 1); a word is whatever width the architecture works with natively. Beyond a byte, capacity scales in powers of 1024 (KB, MB, GB, TB, PB).

prompt: In symmetric multiprocessing (SMP), how are the processors organized?
choices: Each processor is assigned a specific, predefined task
Every processor is a peer, each running its own copy of the OS and sharing memory
One master processor controls several subordinate processors
Each processor is a separate machine linked by a SAN
solution: In SMP every processor is a peer: each runs an identical copy of the OS, can perform any task, and all share the same memory. Asymmetric multiprocessing instead assigns each processor a specific task.

prompt: Clustered systems typically share storage through a ___ to provide high-availability service.
choices: system bus
storage-area network (SAN)
interrupt vector
device controller
solution: A cluster links multiple complete computers that share storage over a storage-area network (SAN), delivering high-availability service that survives a node failure. Clustering can be asymmetric (a hot-standby node) or symmetric (all nodes active).

prompt: What most directly distinguishes multitasking (timesharing) from plain multiprogramming?
choices: It requires multiple CPUs
The CPU switches jobs frequently enough for interactive, real-time response
It eliminates the need for scheduling
It keeps only one job in memory at a time
solution: Both keep the CPU busy by switching among jobs. Multitasking is the interactive extension: the CPU switches so frequently that a user can interact with each program while it runs (response times under ~1 second).

prompt: In one sentence, distinguish job scheduling from CPU scheduling.
answer: Job scheduling decides which jobs are loaded into memory to run; CPU scheduling decides which of the ready, in-memory processes runs on the CPU next.
solution: Job scheduling operates at the boundary of memory — choosing which jobs from the pool get brought in. CPU scheduling operates among the processes already in memory — choosing which ready process gets the CPU next.

prompt: A trap (or exception) is a hardware interrupt raised by an external device.
solution: False. A trap (or exception) is a SOFTWARE interrupt, triggered by the running program itself — either by an error (e.g. division by zero) or by a deliberate request for an OS service (a system call). Hardware interrupts come from devices.

prompt: The interrupt vector is best described as:
choices: A register that stores the mode bit
A table holding the addresses of the interrupt service routines
The signal a device sends to the CPU over the bus
A queue of processes waiting for the CPU
solution: The interrupt vector is a table of the addresses of all interrupt service routines. On an interrupt, control transfers through this table to the right handler — after the hardware first saves the address of the interrupted instruction so execution can resume.

prompt: How does Direct Memory Access (DMA) make high-speed I/O efficient?
choices: It interrupts the CPU once for every byte transferred
The CPU sets up the transfer, then the DMA controller moves an entire block, raising just one interrupt per block
It removes the need for device controllers entirely
It stores transferred data in CPU registers instead of memory
solution: Plain interrupt-driven I/O would interrupt the CPU for every byte. With DMA the CPU only sets up the transfer; the DMA controller then moves a whole block from the device to memory on its own, raising a single interrupt per block. That frees the CPU to do other work — one interrupt per block instead of one per byte.

prompt: What is the purpose of dual-mode (user / kernel) operation?
choices: To make the CPU run faster
To let the OS protect itself and other programs from a misbehaving process
To increase the amount of usable memory
To schedule jobs more fairly
solution: Dual-mode operation exists for protection. Sensitive (privileged) instructions can run only in kernel mode, so a user-mode program can't directly touch hardware or OS state and bring down the system.

prompt: According to the lecture slides, the CPU is executing operating-system (kernel) code when the mode bit is set to:
choices: either value — the bit is unrelated to mode
solution: Lecture slide 34 (and the Silberschatz textbook) set kernel mode = 0 and user mode = 1. Heads-up: the Chapter 1 review deck lists this reversed (0 = user, 1 = kernel). The two course sources disagree on the bit value, so anchor on the CONCEPT — privileged instructions run only in kernel mode — and use whichever convention your specific exam states.
source: Lecture slide 34 (note: review deck reverses the values)

prompt: Dual-mode operation alone can't stop a user program stuck in an infinite loop. How does the OS still regain control?
answer: The OS sets a hardware timer before handing the CPU to user code; when the timer expires it raises an interrupt that returns control to the OS. Setting the timer is a privileged instruction, so user code can't disable it.
solution: A runaway loop never voluntarily returns to the OS, so the OS arms a timer beforehand. The timer interrupt forcibly returns control. Because setting/clearing the timer is a privileged (kernel-mode) instruction, a user program can't switch it off — that's what makes the protection airtight.

prompt: Which of the following is a privileged instruction that user-mode code may NOT execute directly?
choices: Adding two registers
Setting the system timer
Reading a value from memory the program owns
Incrementing a loop counter
solution: I/O control, setting the timer, and interrupt management are privileged operations reserved for kernel mode. Ordinary arithmetic and reading your own memory are not privileged. A user program reaches privileged operations only by making a system call.

prompt: A balanced binary search tree improves worst-case search performance from O(n) to:
choices: O(log n)
O(n log n)
O(n²)
solution: An arbitrary BST can degrade to O(n) in the worst case (a lopsided, list-like tree). Keeping it balanced guarantees the height stays logarithmic, so search is O(log n).

prompt: A bitmap is used to represent:
choices: The addresses of interrupt service routines
The on/off status of each of n items — for example which disk blocks are free
A sorted sequence of processes
The history of the mode bit
solution: A bitmap is a string of n binary digits, each recording the status (on/off, used/free) of one of n items. It's a compact way to track things like which blocks of a disk are currently free.
